KEYWORDS
Distributed Identity, Deep Neural Network, Blockchain, Identity
Audit, Intellectual Property Protection

As an emerging technical phenomenon, Generative Artificial Intel-
ligence (GenAI)-enabled applications have awakened a remarkable
requirement of data/model trading, e.g., trading DNNmodelsвҖҷ usage
licenses on the third-party business platform. A typical implemen-
tation approach in model commercialization is Model-as-a-Service
(MaaS), covering areas like natural language processing [26], image
recognition [43], and intelligent decision-making [40]. Unfortu-
nately, DNN model trades are encountered the issue of redistribu-
tions or misappropriate usage in unexpected purposes, e.g., com-
petitors or opponents, once the models are traded or parameters are
illegally revealed. Our research addresses the model identity issue
and consider model identity auditing to be an exploring direction
for protecting model Intellectual Property (IP), being a vital initial
component in model commercialization [6, 19, 22, 29].
An ideal model identity auditing is able to verify the ownership
of DNN model, ensure the integrity of the training process, and
validate the training parameters to prevent unauthorized data theft
or redistribution. However, existing DNN IP protection schemes are
unable to comply with the requirement of model identity auditing.
Current schemes can be typically categorized into three types [9].
The first type mainly identifies and recognizes DNN models, e.g.,
watermarking and fingerprinting techniques. To be specific, model
watermarking embeds secret watermarks into DNN models by us-
ing covert modifications, such as altering model weights, activation
functions, or network structures. Even though these subtle changes
to the model structure are unique and stable to verify the modelвҖҷs
identity, the modelвҖҷs performance will be degraded [32, 42, 45]. In
addition, model fingerprinting extracts features or metadata from
the model to generate the unique identifier. A vital weakness of

2.1  Commitment Scheme
The commitment scheme is a crucial cryptographic primitive that
involves a two-party interactive protocol (prover, verifier). In the
interactive protocol, the parties agree on a value and generate a
commitment, which ensures the privacy of the secret value while
allowing for potential disclosure by prover. A typical commitment
scheme CS consists of three Probabilistic Polynomial Time (PPT)
algorithms CS = (CS.Gen, CS.Commit, CS.Open):

prover sends the commitment com to the verifier. Hence,
the verifier remains oblivious to any valuable information

3.1  Threat Model for A2-DIDM
Our proposed A2-DIDM can verify the model identity integrity
of the final DNN model training process. Compared with chang-
ing the training structure or iterative optimization algorithm of
DNN, A2-DIDM only requires model owners to generate addi-
tional accumulator proofs to the model weight checkpoints W =
[рқ‘Ҡ0,рқ‘Ҡ1,рқ‘Ҡ2, ...,рқ‘Ҡрқ‘ғ ]. To be specific, during the DNN training pro-
cess, the owner P discloses the weight checkpoints W at intervals,
and synchronously updates the accumulator proof value recorded
in IR. Once P claims its ownership of the final DNN model рқ‘“рқ‘Ҡрқ‘ғ ,
the trusted third party V verifies the correctness of proofs in IR,
which generated on datasets D and model weight checkpoints W
via accumulator.
In order to seize ownership of the model, adversary A aims to
deceive V by falsely claiming that it performes the computation
required to train the final DNN model рқ‘“рқ‘Ҡрқ‘ғ . We assume that A has
white-box access to the рқ‘“рқ‘Ҡрқ‘ғ structure and weights, and may even
has unlimited access to a labeled auxiliary dataset, denoted as D  ,

3.2  Distributed Identity Construction of DNN
Model
In our proposed A2-DIDM, the authorized model owners with ac-
cess to blockchain can execute DNN training computation off-chain.
Subsequently, a series of IRs, include the weight checkpoint se-
quences, are recorded in privacy-preserving and publicly-verifiable
transactions on-chain, ensuring the integrity and correctness of the
training executions.

intermediate weight checkpoint of the DNN model, DIDM can

generate a DIDM, which includes three main phases, which are
Parameter Initialization, Key Pair Generation and IR Generation.
In Parameter Initialization, the public parameters pp = ( ppрқҗ» ,
ppрқҗ¶, ppрқ‘Қрқҗҫ ) are initialized to generate commit proofs for IRs and can
be called by all other algorithms. Specifically, ppрқҗ» is utilized to facil-
itate the computation of a collision-resistant hash function within
the polynomial commitment scheme. The parameters ppрқҗ¶ and ppрқ‘Қрқҗҫ
are generated by DIDM.ComPPGen(В·) and DIDM.ZKPPGen(В·), re-
spectively. We note that the execution of DIDM.ComPPGen(В·) algo-
rithm aligns with the PCS.Gen(В·) algorithm in its implementation.
Similarly, DIDM.ZKPPGen(В·) utilizes NIZK.Gen(В·) algorithm from
NIZK arguments. In Key Pair Generation, the key pair denotes the
owner of the IR. P utilizes parameters, e.g., predicates, public key
address, and commitment randomness, to compute a commitment
CP for each element in W. In IR Generation, DIDM.IRCommit(В·)
adopts PCS.Commit(В·) to ensure the validation of IR commitment
CPрқ‘– . PCS possesses both binding and hiding properties.
3.3  Accumulator-based Audit for DIDM
In A2-DIDM, the blockchain traces and tracks the IR of DNN
model. A complete DIDM is represented by a collection of IRs,
and three predicates control the uniqueness of the DNN weight
checkpoint sequence. P performs DNN training off-chain, and
then submits transactions to blockchain for verification, which
include the training history (i.e., weight checkpoint sequences).
The execution correctness is ensured by efficiently opening new
IR commitments, which allows for the validation of the performed
computations. Additionally, the computational integrity is captured
in the satisfiability of three predicates for all new IRs.

To maintain the zero-knowledge property of weight data and
predicates, the model identity audit in A2-DIDM utilizes two zk-
SNARK provers. For new IRs, A2-DIDM employs an internal zk-
SNARK prover to demonstrate the satisfiability of predicates. An
external zkSNARK prover is utilized to prove the correctness of
all inner proofs. Figure 3 shows the construction of two zkSNARK
provers. Commitment denotes the zkSNARK proofs for IRs and
predicates. The user generates inner proofs to prove that all rele-

that P has completely executed the DNN training calculation. To
ensure function privacy, the SNARK proofs of predicates are used to
generate outer proofs рқңӢрқ‘ңрқ‘ўрқ‘Ў within the external zkSNARK provers.

In the context of SNARKs, polynomial commitment schemes of-
ten involve expensive pairing operations. To reduce the verification
costs for the SNARK verifier, we introduce the accumulator into
the external zkSNARK prover. Figure 4 shows the the construction
of accumulator-based SNARK scheme. The A2-DIDM prover only
needs to embed the logic of the accumulator prover into the circuit.
Then, the external zkSNARK prover will accept an additional accu-
mulator proof tuple (рқҗҙрқҗ¶рқҗ¶рқ‘Ҳрқ‘–рқ‘ӣ,рқңӢрқ‘–рқ‘ӣ) as the input of the accumulator
prover. In addition, the verifier requires an additional decider
algorithm to verify the correctness of the accumulator proof. The
accumulator prover is used to calculate the group element for ef-
fective opening of polynomial commitment. On the other hand, the
accumulator verifier is utilized to validate the correctness of the
proverвҖҷs computations. Moreover, with the decider algorithm, we
move a few expensive pairing operations from the SNARK verifier
to the accumulator verifier on the blockchain.

4.1  Security of Commitment Scheme
Correctness of A2-DIDM commitment scheme. Correctness de-
notes the property that the commitment computed by any honest
worker is verifiable with a significant degree of certainty. Specifi-
cally, when a user wants to expose one or some of the attributes in
a commitment, the commitment verifier can verify the consistency
of these open values with their original committed values without
relying on the proofs provided by the prover. Specifically, all al-
gorithms in A2-DIDM should be executed correctly, which means
that model weight checkpoints with honestly-generated private
keys should be successfully verified. Correctness formally requires
as follows:

4.3  Security of IR and Predicates
Refer to the threat model and the two properties of effective proof,
we define three attacks by adversaries with capabilities in our threat
model. Our following discussion shows that none of them can defeat
A2-DIDM under our threat model.
Data privacy and function privacy. Since the A owns the
labeled dataset рқҗ·рқ‘Һрқ‘‘рқ‘Ј , A can manipulate the training data to fal-
sify the model identity. Synthetic data or data reordering results
in a non-unique training process for the final DNN model, thereby
compromising the integrity of identity audit (M1). However, adver-
saryвҖҷs synthesized weight checkpoint sequence is prone to sudden
jumps in the specification of weight changes, causing the verifier
to mark the checkpoint as suspicious via рқңҷрқҗ¶рқ‘Ҡрқҗ¶рқҗ·. Moreover, due
to the binding and hiding properties, CP denotes commitment to all
attributes of IR without revealing any information where guaran-
tee the data privacy. And the relationship of predicates are hiding
via external SNARK prover, which ensures the function privacy.
The SNARK proving system therefore guarantees that an adversary
cannot construct synthetic data points to produce a specific gradi-
ent because it does not know the corresponding weightрқ‘Ҡ when
selecting data point рқҗ·.
Security of distance from initial weight to final weight.
By placingрқ‘Ҡ0 in a favorable position, A can steer the DNN train-
ing process towards its desired final result рқ‘“рқ‘Ҡрқ‘ғ , thereby forging a
training history to cheat the verifier (M2). However, рқңҷрқҗјрқ‘Ҡрқҗ№рқ‘Ҡ en-
sures every weight checkpoint sequence Wt (рқ‘Ў вҲҲ [рқ‘Ү ]) of the DNN
model has been randomly initialized from GMM distribution, while
it also guarantees the the independence between any two weight
parameters of the initial model.
Security of monotonicity of weight checkpoint accuracy.
An adversary can construct a sequence of weight checkpoints that
deceives validators (M3). While creating fake checkpoints may
be straightforward, the authenticity of randomly generated check-
points is often questionable. Hence, the predicateрқңҷрқ‘Җрқ‘Ҡрқҗ¶рқҗҙ serves the
purpose of verifying the monotonicity of the model weight check-
points, thereby ensuring the genuineness of the ownerвҖҷs checkpoint
sequence. In addition, the correctness and soundness of the com-
mitment scheme provides the security guarantee of the weight
checkpoint commitments, and it is not feasible to forge commit-
ment proofs.

Compared with Pedersen Commitment. We compared A2-
DIDMcommitment scheme to Pedersen commitment and summarize
the experiment results in Table 3. We evaluate A2-DIDM and Ped-
ersen commitment in terms of Init time, Parameter generation time,

The Prover time in SNARK is primarily determined by the execu-
tion of multiple multi-exponentiations and Fast Fourier Transforms
(FFTs), with the size of FFTs being linearly proportional to R1CS
constraints. Our A2-DIDM commitment scheme demonstrates sig-
nificantly higher efficiency in prover compared to Pedersen com-
mitment. By utilizing A2-DIDM with an accumulator-based prover
and Poseidon-128 hash function, the efficiency of the prover has an
enhancement of approximately 80% in comparison to employing a
Pedersen hash function with the same tree height в„Һ. This improve-
ment is attributed to the difference in constraint usage between
the Poseidon-128 and Pedersen hash functions within the SNARK
construction. Specifically, for each hash function call inside the
SNARK, the Poseidon-128 hash function only requires 316 R1CS
constraints, while the Pedersen hash function requires a signifi-
cantly higher number of constraints, specifically 2753 constraints
per call. Consequently, when generating commitment proofs for a
tree of the same height в„Һ, A2-DIDM with the Poseidon-128 hash
function achieves higher efficiency as it involves fewer constraints
and computations.
In addition, In the case of synthesize time for рқ‘Ҷрқ‘–рқ‘§рқ‘’ = 64 proofs
under в„Һ = 5, it takes 3.437s to synthesize a circuit into SNARK
proving system and 5.150s to synthesize in A2-DIDM. Despite the
increased synthesize time cost of A2-DIDM, the reduction in Prover
time costs and Parameter generation time of A2-DIDM reflects a
substantial enhancement in overall performance, considering the
total time required for prover and verifier.

cess to identify the distributed identity of the DNN model, without
exposing the ownerвҖҷs private dataset. Additionally, it is difficult for
adversaries without access to the trained model to steal or forge
the training process of the DNN model. Therefore, our proposed

verification methods to secure the innovations and creative endeav-
ors in this field. One of exploration directions for protecting model
ownership is model watermarking. To protect the model copyright,
the model owner embeds a watermark into the model which is
unknown to the attackers. For example, Adi et al. [1] propose a
backdoor and commitment scheme-based public watermarking al-
gorithm, which cryptographically models deep neural networks
without losing the credibility of the proof. SSLGuard [8] is a water-
marking scheme specifically developed for pre-trained encoders in
self-supervised learning. It introduces watermarks that contain both
validation dataset and secret vector information, thereby preserv-
ing the integrity of the clean encoder while embedding the desired
watermark. IPGuard [4] utilizes data samples in the proximity of
the decision boundary of the original model as trigger data. Wen et
al. [36] couples enhanced watermarking with the DNN model, uti-
lizing watermark triggers generated from original training samples
to combat retraining forgetting.
However, model watermarking technology usually has a trade-
off between practicability and robustness, and requires correspond-
ing modification of the training algorithm or model structure. In
addition, model stealing attacks may remove watermarks or in-
validate watermarks to infringe model copyrights [17, 31, 35].

To
protect model ownership from model stealing attacks, Guan et al.
[15] introduce sample correlation into model ownership protection
to identify model stealing attacks by correlation difference as a
robustness indicator. Li et al. [21] identify victim models via exoge-
nous feature embedding and meta-classifier training. Combining
improvements in proxy networks, Mazeika et al. [25] propose a
gradient redirection defense that alters the trajectory of extraction
attacks. To achieve IP protection, the concept of model identity
has been proposed in RAI2[9] and SoK[5]. Model identity auditing
can support effective governance of AI models, such as protecting
the legitimate rights of model owners or supporting regulatory
authorities in reviewing copyright infringement cases or tracing
harmful DNN models.
Verifiable Computing in Cryptography. Verifiable Comput-
ing aims to outsource computing tasks to third-party computing
power providers. The (untrusted) third-party computing power
providers need to complete the computing task and also need to
provide proof of the correctness of the computing result [14, 37]..

The issue of protecting model intellectual property arising from
the commercialization of models has made model ownership audit-
ing an important component. We have introduced accumulators
and the SNARK prooving system into DIDM auditing, and pro-
posed the privacy-preserving A2-DIDM. By leveraging blockchain
technology, the model owner can perform DNN training compu-
tations off-chain, while on-chain lightweight auditing operations
are employed to verify the computational integrity and correctness,
without revealing any specific information of DNN training. Ad-
ditionally, we perform experiments to assess the effectiveness and
usability of A2-DIDM for privacy protection.
